data.reasoningText:
The user wants to enable hot reload (dotnet watch) for their test project permanently without setting an environment variable each time. For `dotnet test`, hot reload is done via `dotnet watch test`. But the user mentions not wanting to set an environment variable - this likely refers to `DOTNET_WATCH_RESTART_ON_RUDE_EDIT` or the `DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER` or similar.

Actually, I think the user is referring to `DOTNET_WATCH` or more likely they want to use `dotnet watch test` but there might be a specific environment variable needed. 

Wait - in .NET 9, for `dotnet test` with the testing platform, hot reload support can be configured. The `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport` is already set to true.


I'm realizing the user might be looking for the `--hotreload` flag or the `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` environment variable that works with the Microsoft Testing Platform. Since this project uses `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport`, they probably want to configure hot reload through that mechanism rather than repeatedly setting environment variables. The user wants hot reload enabled without manually setting environment variables each time. I could configure it directly in the project file using an MSBuild property like `<TestingPlatformHotReload>true</TestingPlatformHotReload>`, or persist the environment variable through a `.env` file or project configuration so it's always active. The `--hotreload` flag is actually a runtime option rather than an MSBuild property, so I need to think about the most practical way to enable this by default. Using `dotnet watch test` with proper configuration in the project file or a `.runsettings` file would be more reliable than trying to set an MSBuild property that might not exist. Let me focus on the simplest approach—either configuring environment variables through the project file or using a `.runsettings` file to persist the hot reload settings. For the Microsoft Testing Platform's native hot reload, I need to either pass `--hotreload` at runtime or set the environment variable. The cleanest permanent solution would be using a `.runsettings` file or creating a wrapper script, since there isn't a standard MSBuild property to configure this directly in the project file. I'm realizing the environment variable approach might not be the cleanest solution here. For the Microsoft Testing Platform with hot reload support, I should set the `TestingPlatformHotReload` property directly in the project file to enable it by default.

Setting MSBuild property... I'm going back and forth on the exact property name here—it could be `TestingPlatformHotReload`, `HotReload`, or something set through environment variables in the project file. The most straightforward approach would be to check what `dotnet test --help` shows for available options, then configure it at the project level so it's always enabled without needing CLI flags.

Looking at this more carefully, since the project has `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport` enabled, the hot reload feature is controlled either through the `--hotreload` command-line flag or the `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` environment variable. There doesn't appear to be a dedicated MSBuild property to automatically enable it, so I'd need to set it via one of those two mechanisms instead. Description:
  .NET Test Driver

Usage:
  dotnet test [options] [[--] <additional arguments>...]]

Options:
  -s, --settings <SETTINGS_FILE>       The settings file to use when running tests.
  -t, --list-tests                     List the discovered tests instead of running the tests.
  -e, --environment <NAME="VALUE">     Sets the value of an environment variable. 
                                       Creates the variable if it does not exist, overrides if it does. 
                                       This will force the tests to be run in an isolated process. 
                                       This argument can be specified multiple times to provide multiple variables.
  
                                       Examples:
                                       -e VARIABLE=abc
                                       -e VARIABLE="value with spaces"
                                       -e VARIABLE="value;seperated with;semicolons"
                                       -e VAR1=abc -e VAR2=def -e VAR3=ghi
  --filter <EXPRESSION>                Run tests that match the given expression.
                                                                               Examples:
                                                                               Run tests with priority set to 1: 
                                       --filter "Priority = 1"
                                                                               Run a test with the specified full name: 
                                       --filter "FullyQualifiedName=Namespace.ClassName.MethodName"
                                                                               Run tests that contain the specified 
                                       name: --filter "FullyQualifiedName~Namespace.Class"
                                                                               See https://aka.ms/vstest-filtering for 
                                       more information on filtering support.
  --test-adapter-path <ADAPTER_PATH>   The path to the custom adapters to use for the test run.
  -l, --logger <LOGGER>                The logger to use for test results.
                                                                               Examples:
                                                                               Log in trx format using a unique file 
                                       name: --logger trx
                                                                               Log in trx format using the specified 
                                       file name: --logger "trx;LogFileName=<TestResults.trx>"
                                                                               See https://aka.ms/vstest-report for 
                                       more information on logger arguments.
  -o, --output <OUTPUT_DIR>            The output directory to place built artifacts in.
  --artifacts-path <ARTIFACTS_DIR>     The artifacts path. All output from the project, including build, publish, and 
                                       pack output, will go in subfolders under the specified path.
  -d, --diag <LOG_FILE>                Enable verbose logging to the specified file.
  --no-build                           Do not build the project before testing. Implies --no-restore.
  --results-directory <RESULTS_DIR>    The directory where the test results will be placed.
                                       The specified directory will be created if it does not exist.
  --collect <DATA_COLLECTOR_NAME>      The friendly name of the data collector to use for the test run.
                                                                               More info here: 
                                       https://aka.ms/vstest-collect
  --blame                              Runs the tests in blame mode. This option is helpful in isolating problematic 
                                       tests that cause the test host to crash or hang, but it does not create a memory 
                                       dump by default. 
  
                                       When a crash is detected, it creates an sequence file in 
                                       TestResults/guid/guid_Sequence.xml that captures the order of tests that were 
                                       run before the crash.
  
                                       Based on the additional settings, hang dump or crash dump can also be collected.
  
                                       Example: 
                                         Timeout the test run when test takes more than the default timeout of 1 hour,

Description:
  
  
  Environment variables:
  
    DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER
    When set to '1' or 'true', dotnet-watch will poll the file system for
    changes. This is required for some file systems, such as network shares,
    Docker mounted volumes, and other virtual file systems.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH
    dotnet-watch sets this variable to '1' on all child processes launched.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH_ITERATION
    dotnet-watch sets this variable to '1' and increments by one each time
    a file is changed and the command is restarted.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH_SUPPRESS_EMOJIS
    When set to '1' or 'true', dotnet-watch will not show emojis in the 
    console output.
  
  Remarks:
    The special option '--' is used to delimit the end of the options and
    the beginning of arguments that will be passed to the child dotnet process.
  
    For example: dotnet watch -- --verbose run
  
    Even though '--verbose' is an option dotnet-watch supports, the use of '--'
    indicates that '--verbose' should be treated instead as an argument for
    dotnet-run.
  
  Examples:
    dotnet watch run
    dotnet watch test
  
      

Usage:
  dotnet-watch [options] [[--] <additional arguments>...]]

data.reasoningText:
OK, so the standard approach for hot reload with tests is `dotnet watch test`. The user wants this to always be enabled without setting environment variables.

Looking at the `dotnet watch` help, it mentions `DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER` as the main env var.

But wait, the user said "hot reload" specifically. In .NET 9, `dotnet watch` supports hot reload out of the box. The question is - what environment variable is the user talking about?

Possible environment variables:
- `DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER` - for polling-based file watching


- `DOTNET_WATCH_SUPPRESS_EMOJIS` - for emoji suppression
- `DOTNET_WATCH_RESTART_ON_RUDE_EDIT` - for handling certain code changes
- `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` - specifically for test hot reload
